title: Denied Exploit Attempts at OT DMZ Boundary

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for SR 5.2.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that IEC 62443 SR 5.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.15.34: Denied Exploit Attempts at OT DMZ Boundary.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.15.34' running on pan:threat, nozomi:alerts, claroty:alert, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=ot_sec earliest=-24h (sourcetype="pan:threat" OR sourcetype="nozomi:alerts" OR sourcetype="claroty:alert")
| eval severity=coalesce(severity, priority, level)
| where action="blocked" OR disposition="blocked" OR threat_id!="none"
| stats count by signature, src_ip, dest_zone, sourcetype
| sort - count
description: Aggregates blocked threats at OT firewall and IDS boundaries to prove active zone boundary protection.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for IEC 62443 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes ot_sec and sourcetypes claroty:alert, nozomi:alerts, pan:threat. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Normalize severity scales; (2) enrich with MITRE if desired; (3) tune noisy signatures; (4) daily SOC rollup for management. Indexes required: `index=ot_sec`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=claroty:alert`, `sourcetype=nozomi:alerts`, `sourcetype=pan:threat`.
knownFalsePositives: Zone communications during authorized engineering, vendor maintenance, or planned commissioning.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), ES (optional).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `pan:threat`, `nozomi:alerts`, `claroty:alert`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Normalize severity scales; (2) enrich with MITRE if desired; (3) tune noisy signatures; (4) daily SOC rollup for management.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot_sec earliest=-24h (sourcetype="pan:threat" OR sourcetype="nozomi:alerts" OR sourcetype="claroty:alert")
| eval severity=coalesce(severity, priority, level)
| where action="blocked" OR disposition="blocked" OR threat_id!="none"
| stats count by signature, src_ip, dest_zone, sourcetype
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Denied Exploit Attempts at OT DMZ Boundary (IEC 62443-3-3 / SR 5.2)** — Aggregates blocked threats at OT firewall and IDS boundaries to prove active zone boundary protection.

Documented **Data sources**: `pan:threat`, `nozomi:alerts`, `claroty:alert`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), ES (optional). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot_sec; **sourcetype**: pan:threat, nozomi:alerts, claroty:alert. Those sourcetypes align with what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ot_sec, sourcetype="pan:threat", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **severity** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where action="blocked" OR disposition="blocked" OR threat_id!="none"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by signature, src_ip, dest_zone, sourcetype** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with Cyber Vision / Nozomi (or your industrial IDS console) and the same traffic slice. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar (top signatures), Map (src geo), Table.
grandmaExplanation: We aggregates blocked threats at OT firewall and IDS boundaries to prove active zone boundary protection. That helps protect factories, plants, and the gear that runs them from mistakes and bad actors the standards call out.
